trade.summary: China is Sweden's largest import source from Asia. Sweden exports machinery, vehicles (Volvo), pharmaceuticals, and telecommunications equipment. China exports electronics, machinery, and consumer goods. Swedish companies have significant China operations but political tensions have dampened trade growth.
disputes: China retaliated by restricting Swedish telecom company Ericsson in China
Detained Swedish bookseller Gui Minhai case

diplomatic.summary: Relations at historic low. Sweden closed its Confucius Institutes, banned Huawei from 5G, and has been one of Europe's most vocal critics of China. China detained Swedish national Gui Minhai (bookseller), triggering Swedish outrage. Sweden expelled Chinese ambassador temporarily and summoned Chinese diplomats repeatedly. Sweden's 2024 NATO accession deepened the rupture.
recent_incidents: China detained Swedish bookseller Gui Minhai; he remains imprisoned
Sweden banned Huawei from 5G; China retaliated against Ericsson
Sweden joined NATO (2024), further straining ties

military.summary: Sweden is now a NATO member (2024). China is designated a systemic challenge by NATO. No military cooperation. Sweden has strongly supported Ukraine and condemned China's Russia ties. Swedish defense establishment views China as a security threat.
incidents: Sweden has supported EU sanctions aligned positions on China

financial.summary: Swedish companies (Volvo, Ericsson, IKEA) have significant China operations, creating economic interdependence. However, Ericsson lost major Chinese contracts in retaliation for Huawei ban. Swedish FDI in China declining as companies de-risk. Chinese investment in Sweden is being scrutinized.

energy.summary: Sweden imports Chinese solar panels and battery components. Limited direct energy trade. Sweden's clean energy transition relies partly on Chinese clean tech supply chains.

scientific.summary: Scientific cooperation has declined significantly. Confucius Institutes closed. Swedish universities have reviewed and reduced Chinese partnerships. Security concerns about Chinese academic espionage are high in Sweden. Joint publications declining.
